label: Free Will
definition: The question of whether and in what sense agents can act freely and be morally responsible for their actions, given that the world may be wholly governed by prior causes, and the long debate over whether freedom and determinism can be reconciled.

body.text: Free will is the capacity an agent is supposed to have to choose and act otherwise than it does, in a way that makes the agent the genuine origin of its actions and a fit target for praise and blame. The problem of free will is the tension between this capacity and the apparent fact that everything that happens, including every human choice, is the product of prior causes operating under natural law. If my decision was fixed by the state of the universe before I was born, in what sense was it up to me? The debate is ancient, running through the Stoics, Augustine, and the medievals, but its modern shape was set in the early modern period by Hobbes, Hume, and Kant, and it remains one of the most actively contested problems in philosophy. The stakes are not academic: moral responsibility, desert, punishment, and the very idea of an agent seem to hang on the answer.

The positions divide first over a single question: are free will and determinism compatible? Incompatibilists say no. Among them, hard determinists (and their modern cousins, hard incompatibilists) accept that determinism, or the mix of determinism and randomness science actually offers, is true and conclude that free will, and with it deep moral responsibility, is an illusion, a position defended in different forms by Derk Pereboom and Galen Strawson. Libertarians, the other kind of incompatibilist, insist free will is real and therefore determinism must be false, holding that some choices are genuinely undetermined and originated by the agent; their challenge is to explain how an undetermined event could be a controlled action rather than mere randomness, which would be no better for responsibility than determinism. Compatibilists, the majority tradition since Hume, argue the whole opposition is confused: to act freely is not to be uncaused but to act from one's own desires and reasons without external compulsion or coercion. On this view a determined choice can still be free, because freedom is the right kind of causal connection between the agent's will and the act, not the absence of causation. Harry Frankfurt refined this by locating freedom in a harmony between first-order desires and the higher-order desires one wants to have.

The objections track the fault lines. Against libertarianism, critics press the "luck" or "randomness" objection: if a choice is undetermined, it seems to happen to the agent rather than being authored by them, so indeterminism appears to undermine control rather than secure it. Against compatibilism, incompatibilists urge that it changes the subject, redefining freedom as mere absence of constraint while leaving untouched the deeper worry that the agent's desires and character were themselves installed by causes the agent did not choose, so the buck never genuinely stops with the agent. Empirical work has been drawn in too: Benjamin Libet's experiments in the 1980s, which found brain activity preceding the conscious awareness of a decision, were taken by some to threaten conscious free will, though the interpretation is heavily disputed. The debate also intertwines with the mind-body-problem, since what room there is for agency depends on how mental causation fits into physical law.

For AI the problem of free will arrives along two routes. The first is conceptual and reflexive: thinking about deterministic or near-deterministic artificial agents sharpens the human question. An AI system's behavior is the product of its weights, its inputs, and its training, a clear case of a complex system acting from internal states without metaphysical origination. If we are nonetheless willing to describe such a system, via the intentional-stance, as choosing and acting for reasons, that willingness pressures the assumption that human freedom requires something more exotic than sophisticated, reasons-responsive causation. Compatibilism, in effect, is the view on which both humans and suitable machines could count as free in the only sense that matters for holding them answerable.

The second route is practical and ethical. Questions of responsibility for AI actions, who is accountable when an autonomous system causes harm, presuppose some account of agency and control, and the free-will debate supplies the conceptual tools. If an AI is a genuine agent acting for reasons, it becomes a candidate for a kind of responsibility; if it is a mere instrument, responsibility flows back to its designers and deployers. This bears on the control-problem, since controllability assumes the agent's behavior is shaped by external intervention rather than spontaneously originated, and on moral-patienthood, since the capacities that might make a system an agent overlap with those that might make it a subject owed consideration. The free-will problem thus frames how we assign credit, blame, and accountability across the widening population of artificial agents, and it forces us to be precise about what we mean when we call any system, human or machine, the author of what it does.
